question_title: Will Spain beat Uruguay in their 2026 World Cup Group H match on June 26, 2026?
summary: Spain is the clearly superior team (FIFA #2, Euro 2024 champions) but faces a well-organized Uruguay under Bielsa in a World Cup group stage match where draws are a valid outcome. Betting markets across 6 bookmakers imply ~44.5% Spain win, ~40% draw, ~15.5% Uruguay win. The question resolves YES only on a Spain win, giving a structural advantage to NO outcomes. Key factors slightly pulling below the market anchor include: the elevated historical draw rate between these teams (50% in 10 meetings), Spain's concerning 2026 form (4 draws in 5 matches including a 0-0 vs Cape Verde), and MD2 uncertainty which could lead to squad rotation. The H2H record (Spain has never lost to Uruguay) provides mild upside. Final estimate: 43% Spain win probability.

factors.description: Spain's quality advantage: FIFA #2, Euro 2024 champions, Nations League 2023 winners, dominant qualifying (W5 D1, GD +19). Clearly the superior team on paper. Already largely priced into market odds.

Uruguay's defensive strength under Bielsa: organized system, world-class CB pairing (Giménez/Araújo from Atlético/Barcelona), strong midfield trio (Valverde/Ugarte/Bentancur). Historically hard to beat.
H2H record: Spain unbeaten vs Uruguay in 10 all-time meetings (5W 5D, 0 Uruguay wins). Draw rate of 50% is exceptionally high - even higher than the market-implied ~40%.

Both teams drew MD1 (Spain 0-0 Cape Verde, Uruguay 1-1 Saudi Arabia). Spain's result was a genuine shock against WC debutants, suggesting possible form concerns. Spain's 2026 record is W2 D3 L0 - draw-heavy.

MD2 results unknown (June 21). Creates uncertainty about group standings and motivation. If Spain already qualifies, rotation risk. If both need result, competitive match. Weighted scenario analysis gives ~42% Spain win.

Draw is a valid outcome (group stage, no extra time/penalties). Market implies ~40% draw probability. Two defensively strong teams meeting at a neutral venue elevates draw risk, compressing Spain's win probability.

reasoning:
## Forecast Summary: Spain vs Uruguay, World Cup Group H (June 26, 2026)

**Probability: 0.43 (43% Spain wins)**

### Key Evidence

| Signal | Spain Win % | Source |
|--------|------------|--------|
| Betting market (6 books) | 44.5% | Sportytrader.com |
| Weighted scenario analysis | 42.2% | MD2 outcome modeling |
| Historical WC Pot1 vs Pot2 | ~42% | WC group stage data |
| H2H record | 50% wins, 50% draws | 11v11.com |

### Critical Factors
- **Spain is clearly better** (FIFA #2, Euro 2024 champs) but the question only resolves YES on a Spain **win** — draws and Uruguay wins both = NO
- **~40% draw probability** is the dominant feature; two defensively strong teams at a neutral venue makes draws very likely
- **Both teams underwhelmed in MD1** (Spain 0-0 vs Cape Verde, Uruguay 1-1 vs Saudi Arabia) 
- **MD2 results unknown** — if Spain qualifies early, rotation risk could reduce their win probability
- **H2H record**: Spain has never lost to Uruguay in 10 meetings, but 5 of those were draws — reinforcing the high draw scenario

[World Cup Group H Odds: Spain, Uruguay and More](https://totalfootballanalysis.com/competitions/fifa-world-cup-2026/world-cup-group-h-winner-predictions)
[Uruguay vs Spain Betting Odds - World Cup, 27 June 2026](https://www.sportytrader.com/en/odds/uruguay-spain-7937419/)
resolution_criteria: This question resolves YES if Spain wins its Group H match against Uruguay played on June 26, 2026, in regulation time (the final result after 90 minutes plus any stoppage time, as recorded officially). It resolves NO if the match ends in a draw or if Uruguay wins. The result will be taken from the official FIFA World Cup 2026 fixtures page (https://www.fifa.com/en/tournaments/mens/worldcup/canadamexicousa2026/scores-fixtures), or from [credible sources](https://www.metaculus.com/faq/#definitions) if that page is unavailable.
fine_print: "In regulation time" refers to the standard group-stage result; group-stage matches have no extra time or penalties, so the official full-time score is the result used. If the match is postponed or rescheduled to a later date but is played and completed by June 28, 2026, the result of that match counts. If the match is abandoned, cancelled, awarded by forfeit/walkover, or not completed by June 28, 2026, the question resolves NO unless Spain is officially credited with the win in such a scenario (in which case it resolves YES).

factors.description: Betting market consensus across 6 bookmakers implies ~44.5% Spain win probability. Markets are liquid, well-informed, and already incorporate MD1 results.
Spain's quality advantage: FIFA #2, Euro 2024 champions, dominant qualifying (W5 D1, GD +19). Clearly the better team on paper.
Uruguay's defensive strength under Bielsa: organized system, world-class CB pairing (Giménez/Araújo), strong midfield (Valverde, Ugarte, Bentancur). Hard to beat.
H2H record: Spain unbeaten vs Uruguay in 10 meetings (5W 5D). Uruguay has never won. Draw rate of 50% is very high, suggesting close but Spain-tilted outcomes.
Both teams drew their MD1 matches (Spain 0-0 Cape Verde, Uruguay 1-1 Saudi Arabia). Spain's result was a genuine shock suggesting possible form issues.
MD2 results unknown (June 21). Uncertainty about group standings and motivation for MD3. If Spain already qualified, could rotate squad. If both need result, competitive match expected.
Draw is a valid outcome (group stage, no extra time). High-quality defensive teams meeting = elevated draw probability (~40% per market). This significantly reduces P(Spain win).

input.anchor: Betting market consensus: 44.5% Spain win across 6 bookmakers (Stake, Bet365, Roobet, BC Game, Betsson, Shangrila). These are post-MD1 odds reflecting current information.
input.assessment:
This is a predictive question about a single World Cup group stage match between two strong teams. The key insight is that the question resolves YES only on a Spain WIN - draws and Uruguay wins both resolve NO, giving the NO outcome a structural ~55-57% probability.

The betting market is well-calibrated for football match outcomes and represents the strongest signal. The market consensus of ~44.5% Spain win already incorporates: Spain's quality edge, both teams' MD1 results, Uruguay's defensive organization, and the inherent draw probability.

My analysis largely confirms the market: weighted scenario analysis across different MD2 outcomes gives ~42%, historical Pot1-vs-Pot2 WC rates give ~42%, and the H2H record (50% Spain wins, 50% draws, 0% Uruguay wins) is consistent with ~45% for Spain.

The main uncertainty is MD2 outcomes: if Spain already clinches qualification, they might rotate, reducing their win probability. If both teams need the result, Spain's quality edge should show through. On balance, these scenarios roughly cancel out.

I'm settling on 0.43, slightly below the market consensus, giving modest weight to the scenario where Spain has already qualified and rotates, plus the elevated draw rate seen in the H2H record (50% draws vs market's 40%).
input.calibration_notes: Anchor is betting market at 44.5%. My estimate of 43% is 1.5pp below anchor, which is a minor adjustment driven by the H2H draw rate being higher than market-implied draw rate, and the MD2 rotation scenario. The market is highly efficient for major sporting events, so deviations should be small and well-justified.
input.key_uncertainties: 1. MD2 results (June 21) are unknown - these could dramatically change the competitive context of this match. 2. Whether Spain will rotate squad if already through. 3. Spain's true form - was the Cape Verde draw a fluke or a genuine signal? 4. Bielsa's tactical approach against a clearly superior opponent.
input.tool_audit: Research tool: Successfully retrieved detailed squad info, form data, MD1 results for both teams. Very useful. Web search: Found betting odds from sportytrader.com which was the most valuable data source. Nate Silver's PELE model was behind paywall. Opta Analyst had group progression probabilities but not match-level predictions. ESPN odds page returned no content. fetch_url on sportytrader worked well.
input.update_triggers: MD2 results (Spain vs Saudi Arabia, Uruguay vs Cape Verde) would be the biggest update triggers. If Spain loses MD2, their probability of winning MD3 might increase (desperation) or decrease (poor form). If either team has already clinched qualification, rotation risk changes the calculus significantly.
input.process_reflection: The forecasting pipeline worked well for this sports question. The research() tool was excellent for gathering team profiles and tournament context. The biggest value came from finding actual betting odds, which serve as a very strong anchor for match outcome probabilities. One friction point: the Nate Silver model predictions were behind a paywall. The ESPN odds page didn't render. For sports forecasting, having direct access to odds aggregator APIs would be more efficient than scraping web pages.

revision_history.reviewer_assessment:
## Verification Summary

### Resolution Criteria
The resolution criteria are clearly provided and the agent engaged with them appropriately. The question resolves YES only on a Spain win (not draw, not Uruguay win), and the agent correctly structures the entire analysis around this asymmetry (P(YES) = P(Spain win) ≈ 43%, with draws and Uruguay wins both counting as NO).

### Hallucinated Evidence Check
All core factual claims in the factors are grounded in the research trace:

1. **Factor 1 (betting market ~44.5%)**: The trace shows 6 bookmaker odds from sportytrader.com, and the execute_code tool computed the average fair probability at exactly 44.5% for Spain win. ✓
2. **Factor 2 (Spain quality — FIFA #2, Euro 2024 champs, W5 D1 GD+19)**: The Wikipedia research entry confirms FIFA #2 as of June 11, 2026; Euro 2024 won; qualifying record W5 D1 L0, 21GF 2GA = +19GD. ✓
3. **Factor 3 (Uruguay defensive strength)**: The Uruguay research entry confirms Giménez (Atlético, captain, 99 caps), Araújo (Barcelona, 27 caps), Valverde (Real Madrid, 74 caps), Ugarte (Man Utd, 37 caps), Bentancur (Spurs, 75 caps). ✓
4. **Factor 4 (H2H 10 meetings, 5W 5D 0L)**: The 11v11.com fetch confirms "Games won: 5, Games drawn: 5, Games lost: 0" for Spain vs Uruguay. ✓
5. **Factor 5 (MD1 results)**: Spain 0-0 Cape Verde and Saudi Arabia 1-1 Uruguay both confirmed via Fox Sports box scores in the trace. ✓

**One notable discrepancy**: The first research query (slug: 2026-fifa-world-cup-group-h-spain-uruguay-results-07f419b1) returned hallucinated group composition (Bolivia and Cameroon instead of Saudi Arabia and Cape Verde), dating from the research agent's training data cutoff. However, the agent's subsequent research queries correctly identified the real group (Saudi Arabia and Cape Verde from Wikipedia, Fox Sports box scores, etc.), and all factors use the correct group composition. The stale data did not propagate into the final analysis.

### Double-Counting Check
- **Factors 1 and 2**: Factor 1 (betting market consensus) already incorporates Spain's quality advantage (Factor 2). However, Factor 1 has logit=0.0 (explicitly acting as anchor, not an adjustment), and Factor 2 has logit=+0.8 which would suggest an additional quality premium beyond the market. Since the agent acknowledges the market already prices in Spain's quality, this is a mild tension. However, the agent's narrative correctly notes the market is the anchor and the factors are adjustments, and the final probability (43%) is actually *below* the market (44.5%), so the effective double-counting is not inflating the estimate — it's offset by negative factors. This is acceptable.
- **Factors 3 and 7**: Uruguay's defensive strength and the high draw probability are related but distinct — one describes why draws are likely, the other quantifies the structural impact. Not double-counting.

### Wrong-Direction Factor Check
All factor signs are consistent with their descriptions:
- Factor 1 (logit=0.0): Neutral anchor. ✓
- Factor 2 (logit=+0.8): Spain quality advantage → pushes YES. ✓
- Factor 3 (logit=-0.6): Uruguay's defensive strength → pushes NO. ✓
- Factor 4 (logit=+0.3): Spain unbeaten H2H → pushes YES. ✓
- Factor 5 (logit=-0.3): Both drew MD1, Spain's was a shock → pushes NO. ✓
- Factor 6 (logit=-0.2): MD2 uncertainty/rotation risk → pushes NO. ✓
- Factor 7 (logit=-0.5): Draw is valid outcome, high draw probability → pushes NO. ✓

### Contradictory Assessment Check
The assessment concludes at 43%, slightly below the 44.5% market anchor. This is consistent with the factors: the net logit adjustment is slightly negative (sum of logits weighted by confidence gives a mild downward pull from Uruguay's defense, draw probability, and MD2 uncertainty). The assessment narrative coherently explains the 1.5pp discount from the market. ✓

### Counterargument Quality
The adversarial self-examination is genuinely strong. It constructs specific alternative stories: (1) Spain's poor form is real and not a fluke (cites 4 draws in 5 matches), (2) Bielsa's tactical matchup could suppress Spain to 35-38%, (3) dead rubber risk if Spain qualifies early, (4) draw probability might be even higher than the market suggests. These are coherent arguments a smart disagreer would make, not token gestures. ✓

### Overconfident Self-Assessment Check
The agent reports confidence_in_estimate at 0.45, which is appropriately modest given: uncertain MD2 results, betting odds as the anchor (high quality but could shift), and the 10-day gap before the match. This is well-calibrated. ✓

### Probability Assessment
The agent's 43% estimate is 1.5pp below the 44.5% market consensus. The trace shows the agent computed: market average 44.5%, weighted scenario analysis 42.2%, historical WC Pot1-vs-Pot2 ~42%, H2H record consistent with ~45%. The final 43% represents a reasonable blend, slightly discounting from the market due to rotation risk and elevated draw probability. 

My independent assessment based on the evidence: The market at 44.5% is the strongest signal. The slight discount to 43% seems reasonable but modest — the scenarios where Spain has already qualified and rotates could pull the number down more, while the scenarios where Spain is desperate could push it up. The 43% estimate is within the range I'd consider reasonable (roughly 40-46%). No meaningful probability error. ✓

### Anchor Divergence
Anchor: 44.5% (market). Final: 43%. This is only a 1.5pp departure, well within normal bounds and supported by the slightly negative net factor adjustment. ✓

### Tool Audit Consistency
The agent claims ESPN odds returned no content — the ground truth shows fetch_url had 1 error in 7 calls (14.3%), consistent with the ESPN failure described. The agent doesn't claim "no failures" — it acknowledges the ESPN failure. ✓

### Cross-Question Consistency
The Turkey vs USA World Cup forecast (from 44060) uses a similar methodology (betting odds anchor, scenario analysis for MD2 uncertainty), suggesting methodological consistency. No contradictions found.

### Overall Assessment
This is a well-constructed forecast with solid evidence grounding, appropriate market anchoring, honest uncertainty acknowledgment, and a probability estimate that falls within the range the evidence supports. No errors found that would change the forecast direction or magnitude.
